data.title: Shoe Carnival outlines $100M working capital release and $20M cost savings by 2027 as Shoe Station pivot accelerates
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Shoe Carnival, Inc. (SCVL) Q3 2025

Management View

  • Mark Worden, President, CEO & Director, stated, “Last week, we announced that our Board of Directors unanimously approved changing our corporate name to Shoe Station Group, Inc., subject to approval of the name changed by our shareholders at our annual meeting to be held in June 2026. That decision reflects our Board's conviction about where this company is headed. We're building a stronger, more focused and more profitable company.”
  • Worden highlighted, “We delivered a strong third quarter. EPS of $0.53 and net sales of $297.2 million, both exceeded consensus expectations. Gross profit margin expanded 160 basis points to 37.6%, driven by disciplined pricing and our continued shift toward the higher-income Shoe Station customer.”
  • Worden emphasized strategic progress: “We completed 101 store rebanners during fiscal 2025. We now operate 428 stores, 144 Shoe Station locations and 284 Shoe Carnival locations. This evolution started with test and learn, moved to scaling across the Southeast and is now a full chain rollout.”
  • Worden asserted, “By back-to-school 2026, [Shoe Station] will be 51%. That 51% threshold is the inflection point when Shoe Station becomes the majority of this business and we expect to return to comparable sales growth.”
  • W. Jackson, CFO & EVP, reported, “Net sales totaled $297.2 million, down 3.2% versus $306.9 million last year. Comparable store sales declined 2.7%, including approximately 0.5 percentage point of headwind from the 56 stores rebannered during the quarter.”
  • Jackson detailed, “Gross profit margin expanded 160 basis points to 37.6%, exceeding the high end of our guidance. Merchandise margins increased 190 basis points, driven by disciplined pricing, favorable mix shift towards Shoe Stations higher-income consumers and our strategic inventory investments.”

Outlook

  • Jackson stated, “We are reaffirming our net sales guidance and continue to expect net sales of $1.12 billion to $1.15 billion. We are raising the EPS guidance range to $1.80 to $2.10, increasing the low end by $0.10. We continue to expect gross profit margin of 36.5% to 37.5% and now expect SG&A in the range of $350 million to $355 million, down $5 million from previous guidance.”
  • For Q4, Jackson forecasted, “Net sales of $240 million to $270 million, ranging from down 7% to up 2% compared to Q4 last year… We expect Q4 EPS in the range consistent with consensus prior to our earnings release in a range of $0.25 to $0.30. Q4 EPS in that range targets full year EPS at the lower end of our annual outlook.”
  • Looking ahead, Jackson said, “While we're not providing detailed fiscal 2026 guidance today, that will come in March, we can provide transparency on what to expect. Fiscal 2026 will be a year of continued investment. We believe that next year's investments will lead to a return to sales and earnings growth in fiscal 2027 and further accelerating in fiscal 2028 as we complete the rebannering program.”

Financial Results

  • Net sales of $297.2 million were reported for Q3, with comparable store sales down 2.7%.
  • Shoe Station net sales grew 5.3% with mid-single-digit comparable sales growth, while Shoe Carnival net sales declined 5.2%.
  • Gross profit margin reached 37.6%, up 160 basis points.
  • SG&A was $93.2 million or 31.3% of sales.
  • Net income for Q3 was $14.6 million or $0.53 per diluted share, compared to $19.2 million or $0.70 per share last year.
  • The company ended the quarter with over $107 million in cash, cash equivalents and marketable securities, and remains debt-free.

Q&A

  • Mitchel Kummetz, Seaport Research Partners: “I think you said that for this year, the drag on earnings is $0.68 to $0.70. Can you say what the additional drag will be next year? And then help us kind of think through what happens in 2027 and '28.” W. Jackson responded, “We said $25 million to $30 million in rebanner expenses are expected next year… they're going to be front-loaded because we're going to be doing approximately 70 stores… we're expecting to get those monies back in a 2- to 3-year time frame in the profitability of those stores post conversion.”
  • Kummetz asked about stripping out rebannering expenses for core earnings. Jackson clarified, “Next year, we're going to be an investment year… we expect sales to be down in the first half. Now once at back-to-school, once we hit that 51% threshold where Shoe Station is the dominant brand, we expect to see a flat to slight positive sales gain in the second half.”
  • Kummetz asked about boot performance. Tanya Gordon, EVP & CMO, said, “Boots did start a little bit slow. But as we got our inventory in… we saw nice double-digit increases. So bodes well as we move into fourth quarter.”
  • Samuel Poser, Williams Trading: “So as I understand it, at the end of the third quarter last year, I believe there were 42 Shoe Station stores. And today, there are 144. Is that correct?” Jackson replied, “The 144 is correct. I'll have to double check -- the 42 is approximately right.”
  • Poser inquired about inventory reduction strategy. Worden explained, “We're going to clear it. We're not going to carry it if it's not a go-forward in the Shoe Station, that will have some margin pressure. It's the right thing to do.”
  • James Chartier, Monness, Crespi, Hardt: “You previously talked about getting to 80% of stores rebannered by March of 2027. Is that still the plan?” Worden stated, “We will be well over 90% before we finish 2028, and we will surpass the critical point of 51% this summer.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts probed persistently for detail on the earnings drag from rebannering and the timing of margin and sales inflection, with a slightly negative to neutral tone, reflected in repeated questions on the speed of cost recovery and pressure on gross margin.
  • Management maintained a confident and explanatory tone in prepared remarks and Q&A, citing “conviction that this strategy is right for long-term value creation” and repeatedly emphasizing the expected timeline for recovery and growth, while acknowledging near-term headwinds and margin pressure.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, current management sentiment remained confident but less buoyant, with greater focus on near-term investment and transition costs. Analyst questions in the current call were more pointed regarding execution and risk.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The Q3 call introduced a formal plan to change the corporate name to Shoe Station Group, signifying a deeper commitment to single-banner strategy.
  • Operating results in Q3 showed expansion in gross margin and continued divergence between Shoe Station’s growth and Shoe Carnival’s decline, a theme consistent with Q2, but with a larger scale of rebannering and more explicit financial impact on EPS.
  • The outlook for 2026 is now described as an “investment year” with lower earnings expected, as opposed to the more optimistic tone in Q2, which focused on margin expansion and comp recovery.
  • Analyst focus shifted from margin integrity and inventory strategy toward more detailed scrutiny of cost recovery, inventory liquidation, and the cadence of margin pressure through the rebannering transition.
  • Management’s confidence in the rebanner strategy remains strong, but guidance language has become more nuanced, stressing the need for patience as the company works through the majority transition.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management noted continued pressure on lower income households, particularly those earning under $40,000 annually, affecting Shoe Carnival sales and prompting the strategic shift toward the higher-income Shoe Station consumer.
  • Margin pressure is anticipated as non-go-forward inventory is cleared aggressively, especially during the conversion of 70 stores in 2026.
  • Macro-economic volatility, consumer behavior uncertainty in nonevent periods, and fourth quarter weather are cited as risks to near-term sales.
  • Analyst concerns centered on the speed of inventory reduction, the cadence of rebannering costs, and the risk of margin dilution from clearing legacy product.

Final Takeaway

Shoe Carnival’s Q3 2025 call underscored an accelerated pivot to the Shoe Station model, with the company planning to surpass 51% of its fleet as Shoe Station by back-to-school 2026 and to exceed 90% by fiscal 2028. Management projects $20 million in annual cost savings and $100 million in working capital release by the end of fiscal 2027, while signaling continued investment and near-term margin pressure as legacy inventory is cleared and store conversions ramp up. The company remains debt-free with cash reserves exceeding $100 million, providing financial flexibility to execute the transition. Investors are guided to expect modest gains beginning in 2027 and meaningful acceleration in 2028, as the transformation to a single-banner business model aimed at higher-income consumers takes hold.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: ChatGPT group chats rolling out globally, OpenAI says
data.description:
• Group chats inside ChatGPT are rolling out globally, OpenAI (OPENAI) said on Thursday, following a successful pilot test.
  • Group chats will be available to “all logged-in users on ChatGPT Free, Go, Plus and Pro plans globally over the coming days,” the company said in a blog post. “We will continue refining the experience as more people start using it.”
  • Group chats allow people to come together to do certain tasks, such as trip planning, and make it possible for all members of a group to understand what ChatGPT is doing at the same time.
  • “It’s also great for group decisions, like finding a restaurant that fits everyone’s tastes or settling a friendly debate with an impartial referee,” the company added. “Group chats also make collaboration at work or school easier. You can draft an outline or research a new topic together. Share articles, notes, and questions, and ChatGPT can help summarize and organize information.”

data.title: Mortgage rates inch up but continue to move in narrow range
data.description:
Mortgage rates edged up but continued to move in a narrow range, according to the latest Freddie Mac (OTCQB:FMCC) Primary Mortgage Survey.

30-year fixed-rate mortgages averaged 6.26% as of November 20, up from 6.24% last week and below the year-ago level of 6.84%.

15-year fixed-rate mortgages averaged 5.54%, up from 5.49% last week and down from 6.02% a year ago.

"Mortgage rates have been shifting within a narrow ten-basis point range over the last month," said Freddie Mac's chief economist, Sam Khater.

"This rate stability is a positive sign for both buyers and sellers, as it helps provide greater certainty in the housing market," added Khater.

Here is a look at the mortgage rate movements in the last one month:

data.title: Wall Street turns red after the Nasdaq's morning 2% rally
data.description:
U.S. stocks gave back early gains on Thursday, erasing a morning rally that had pushed the Nasdaq Composite above 2%.

The tech focused Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was last down by 1%. At the same time, the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) had pushed down by 0.7% and the blue-chip Dow (DJI) dipped by 0.5%. 

From a sector-by-sector point of view, three of the 11 S&P segments find themselves up in positive territory and are led by Consumer Staples. Going in the other direction, Info Tech has been the weakest performer thus far.

As for stocks that were on the move, Nvidia was last -1.7% after adding more than $200B to its market cap with its post-earnings rally, nearing the $200/share level it lost early this month.

Thursday's spotlight was on the September nonfarm payrolls report, which was initially supposed to come out on October 3. However, the longest U.S. government shutdown in history derailed that release.

The data showed the U.S. labor market added 119K jobs in September, easily outstripping the consensus of 50K. The unemployment rate ticked up to 4.4% in the month, a tad higher than the expected 4.3%.

“Thursday's jobs report was much stronger than expected and it's possible that the Federal Reserve may take more of a wait and see approach to rates in December, especially since there is still uncertainty over the economic data, which has only first started to come back online after the drought from the government shutdown. Thursday's jobs report suggests that the labor market is not as weak as feared,” Alexander Guiliano, chief investment officer, Resonate Wealth Partners stated.

Moreover, Treasury yields shifted lower as the longer-end U.S. 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) moved down by 4 basis points to 4.09% and the shorter-end U.S. 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) pushed down by 4 basis points to 3.55%.

data.title: Bitcoin extends rout as U.S. equities reverse Nvidia-driven gains
data.description:
Bitcoin's (BTC-USD) slump deepened Thursday just as U.S. stocks gave back gains fueled by Nvidia's (NVDA) Q3 earnings beat, in a sign of renewed investor caution.

The cryptocurrency retreated 4.7% in the last 24 hours to $87.2K as of 12:12 p.m. ET. Ether (ETH-USD) dropped 6.2% to $2.83K, along with other altcoins like Aster (ASTER-USD) -13%, CyberCoin (CC-USD) -7.8%, Uniswap (UNI-USD) -7.6%, Litecoin (LTC-USD) -6.6% and Cronos (CRO-USD) -6.5%.

At the same time, the stock market wiped out huge opening gains, with all three major indices now in the red.

data.title: Citadel sees S&P 500 reaching 7,000 after recent pullback
data.description:
Scott Rubner, head of equity and equity derivatives strategy at Citadel Securities, predicted a powerful rebound in the S&P 500 (SP500) following what he described as a “healthy” pullback, with the benchmark potentially reaching 7,000 by year-end.

According to the strategist, this target would represent a 5.4% gain from Wednesday’s closing level, driven by a combination of market positioning and favorable seasonal trends.

“The holiday party isn’t canceled — it’s just been moved to a bar,” Rubner wrote in a note to clients on Thursday.

The recent market retreat has created fertile conditions for a strong recovery, with multiple bullish forces converging to support equity prices through December, according to Rubner’s analysis.

These positive catalysts include persistent demand from retail traders and reduced exposure from institutional investors heading into the Thanksgiving holiday, giving these large players more room to rebuild positions.

Also, strong results from Nvidia (NVDA) will likely trigger traders to cover hedges and reestablish positions into year-end, Rubner said.

Historical data strongly supports the case for a year-end rally, he added. Records dating back to 1928 show the S&P 500 (SP500) typically gains approximately 4% from this point through the end of the year, underscoring the favorable seasonal trends now in play.

The benchmark index advanced 0.9% as of Thursday morning trading – however, it later dipped to -0.6% in afternoon trade – while the tech-heavy Nasdaq 100 (COMP:IND) climbed 1.1%, as Nvidia’s (NVDA) robust performance outweighed concerns about the Federal Reserve’s interest-rate path. The former is currently -0.9%, as stocks have pared gains.

Systematic investors represent a key group that Rubner is closely monitoring as they have “clearly entered a de-risking phase,” reducing equity holdings during the recent weakness.

According to the strategist, these mechanical outflows will likely remain substantial over the next few days before completely dissipating, potentially removing a source of selling pressure from the market. Citadel Securities continues to observe strong retail participation, with flows “decisively skewed to the buy side” over the past four weeks.

Retail investors have shown remarkable resilience during the recent volatility, maintaining their position as “one of the most important sources of demand in 2025,” Rubner wrote.

The strategist also observed signs that investor positioning in stocks is broadening beyond this year’s market leaders. While technology (XLK), communication services (XLC), and utilities (XLU) still hold the strongest year-to-date gains, recent performance shows leadership shifting toward health care (XLV), energy (XLE), and consumer-staples (XLP) names, he said, reflecting clients moving toward “broadening-out” trades after months of concentration in the market’s biggest winners.

data.title: The Commerce Dept. will reschedule Q3 GDP, October income/outlays reports
data.description:
The Commerce Department's Bureau of Economic Analysis on Thursday said it will reschedule the releases of its Q3 GDP revision, Q3 corporate profits, and October Personal Income and Outlays reports, but didn't say when they'll be released.

It's notable that the agency rescheduled the data rather than canceled them. On Wednesday, the Bureau of Labor Statistics canceled its October jobs report and its September Job Openings and Labor Turnover Survey, because it couldn't retroactively collect the data.

The BEA's second estimate of Q3 GDP and preliminary corporate profits were originally scheduled for Nov. 26, the same day as it had expected to issue the October Personal Income and Outlays report. The BEA economic release schedule will continue to be updated as information becomes available, it said.

The personal income and outlays data are particularly important because they include the personal consumption expenditure price index, which the Federal Reserve relies on to gauge the inflation rate, especially the core PCE price index, which excludes food and energy.

The Federal Open Market Committee, which determines the Fed's policy rate, next meets on Dec. 9-10.

The BLS has scheduled the November Employment Situation report for Dec. 16, 2025, after the Fed meeting, and the October JOLTS for Dec. 9.

data.title: Nvidia's Q3 results and outlook see largely bullish views from analysts
data.description:
Shares of Nvidia (NVDA) dipped about 1% on Thursday despite an "exceptional" quarter and "stellar" guidance.

The stock gave up its gains of about 5% premarket, which would have seen the company's market cap set to get a boost of over $200B.

"Nvidia Corporation is upgraded to a "cautious Buy" after strong Q3 earnings and stellar Q4 guidance reinforce its growth momentum," said Seeking Alpha analyst Kenio Fontes.

Seeking Alpha analyst Bashar Issa noted that Nvidia delivered "exceptional revenue growth last quarter, fueled by robust demand from generative AI labs and hyperscalers." The analysts maintained his Buy rating on the stock, and said that the company trades at a premium valuation, justified by market leadership, but faces risks if graphics processing unit, or GPU, demand slows or saturation occurs. 

Nvidia's Data Center segment drove 66% year-over-year growth, with robust demand from the U.S. and European clients offsetting lost China revenue due to export restrictions, said Seeking Alpha analyst Agar Capital.

"Despite concerns about valuation and an AI bubble, NVDA's earnings growth outpaces its stock price, resulting in a lower forward P/E and strong justification for a continued rally," the analyst added. Agar Capital kept its Buy rating on the stock.

For the period ending Oct. 26, the Jensen Huang-led company said it earned $1.30 per share as revenue soared 62% year-over-year to come in at $57.01B. Both top and bottom-line numbers beat estimates. Looking to the fourth-quarter of fiscal 2026, Nvidia expects to generate $65B in revenue, plus or minus 2%. Analysts were forecasting $61.98B in revenue.

"Nvidia reported another exceptional quarter with revenues of $57 billion beating estimates by $1.9 billion. The AI GPU company even guided up FQ4 revenues to $65 billion with 75% gross margins, reinforcing the strong AI data center growth and earnings trends," said Seeking Alpha analyst Stone Fox Capital, Investing Group Leader for Out Fox The Street. "The stock isn’t likely to see a huge rally due to these strong earnings only adding to fears of hyperscaler overspending, but the stock is cheap with growth rates topping 50% while the stock trades at only roughly 27x FY27 EPS targets."

The U.S. tech giant's results and guidance drove semiconductor stocks higher premarket on Thursday. Year-to-date Nvidia's stock has surged about 39%. The company has a market cap (intraday) of about $4.537T as of Nov. 20, according to Yahoo Finance.

"Current period guidance was the best news of the day, coming in more than $3 billion ahead of the street's top line expectations," said Seeking Alpha analyst Bill Maurer.

Maurer added that the current valuation is much better than industry peers, even while the growth story is much more impressive.

"Nvidia's revenue growth largely stemmed from its data center business, where Nvidia benefits like no other company from the huge investments by Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL), Meta Platforms (META), Amazon.com (AMZN), and many more. It feels like every week, we get a new announcement about a major data center from these or other tech companies, and Nvidia is providing the chips for most of these data centers – Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) is only a minor competitor, at least for now. Nvidia's data center business contributed around 90% of Nvidia's overall revenues," said Seeking Alpha analyst Jonathan Weber.

However, not all analysts had bullish sentiments.

Nvidia's robust growth is fueled by surging demand for Blackwell GPUs from an expanding (somewhat circular) AI ecosystem, said Seeking Alpha analyst Ahan Vashi from The Quantamental Investor.

"Despite raising revenue forecasts and modeling for aggressive growth, NVDA's valuation implies a -17% downside and a 5-year CAGR return below TQI's investment hurdle rate," said Vashi. The analyst added that "I maintain a Hold rating on NVDA at $197, due to stretched valuation; however, I would consider buying if shares drop to the mid-$100s without business deterioration over the next year."

Meanwhile, Seeking Alpha analyst Kumquat Research rated Nvidia a Sell. The analyst said investors should take a cautious approach and potentially trim positions as competition in the GPU space heats up, and Nvidia's "valuation appears lofty."

"No monopoly with this much reach and that serves customers with pockets as deep as Microsoft, Meta, Apple, and more can last forever, and I think that reckoning is coming for Nvidia even if the AI bubble refuses to burst. As such, I am rating NVDA a Sell," Kumquat Research added.

Update: The story was updated with stock price movement.

data.title: Annexon stock rises after director purchases shares
data.description:
• Annexon (ANNX) stock rose after the company's director disclosed a purchase of shares for an approximate value of about $5M.
  • The stock is up about 15% today.
  • Muneer Satter, director at Annexon, bought about 1.5M shares between a price range of $2.99 and $3.94, as disclosed in SEC filings.

data.title: Sell the rip: market gains evaporate as Nvidia does round trip
data.description:
The morning bull stampede has disappeared as sellers wiped out huge opening gains market gains Thursday.

The Nasdaq (COMP.IND) (QQQ) -0.1% has lost more than 2 percentage points as Nvidia's (NVDA) premarket gains of more than 5% disappeared. The S&P (SP500) (SPY) and Dow (DJI) (DIA) are also in the red. 

Bitcoin (BTC-USD) -3.5% telegraphed risk-off sentiment as it started to decline around 9 a.m. ET, about 2 hours before equities.
